summary: Gruber reviews the 2021 14-inch MacBook Pro, arguing that Apple has corrected years of misplaced priorities by finally making the MacBook Pro truly 'pro.' Despite being technically the same thickness as its predecessor, the new rectilinear design feels more substantial, and the return of MagSafe, HDMI, and SD card slots signals a philosophical shift. He frames the Touch Bar era as minimalism trumping practicality, and sees Apple's correction as letting the truck be a truck. The M1 Max performance is described as impossibly fast, and the 14-inch model now matches the 16-inch spec-for-spec. He concludes it's the nicest and best Mac he's ever used, made possible only by Apple's custom silicon.
key_points: The new MacBook Pro's rectilinear design and added weight make it feel thicker despite identical measurements, evoking the beloved Titanium PowerBook G4
Apple is redefining 'pro' to mean genuinely professional rather than just premium — the Touch Bar era prioritized artistic minimalism over practical utility
The MacBook Air and MacBook Pro had converged too much; Apple needed to push each toward the outer limits of their respective purposes
The 14-inch model is now spec-for-spec identical to the 16-inch, eliminating the historical performance penalty for choosing the smaller size
The notch is initially jarring but fades from notice within a week, and Big Sur's taller menu bar now appears to have been designed with the notch in mind
money_quotes: Apple's best products have always been both tools for work and objects of art. Almost every single change with these new MacBook Pros is in the name of making them better tools for work.

If you're going to make a laptop thin and lightweight, make it really thin and lightweight. And if you're going to make a laptop powerful and practical, make it really powerful and practical. Focus on the outer limits of what's possible, not the boring middle.
Let the truck be a truck, true to its purpose. Let the MacBook Pro be unabashedly pro.
The controversial decisions that went into the Touch-Bar-era MacBooks were in the name of artistic purity. Minimalism trumping practicality. They were out of balance.
Apple does not relish explaining their mistakes. But they do acknowledge them, and make changes to address them. They are confident and proud, but seldom obstinate.
It feels impossibly fast. I simply lack the expertise in any of these areas to adequately evaluate its performance.
It's like meeting an old friend you haven't seen in five years and picking up right where you left off.
key_insight: The 2021 MacBook Pro represents Apple correcting a five-year imbalance where minimalist aesthetics were prioritized over professional utility, finally letting the Pro be unabashedly pro.
